1.description: Rate increases requested and received by NY Life Insurance Co. The figure summarizes the experience of New York Life Insurance Company, one of the largest LTCI companies, with premium increase requests. In panel A, the average premium increase requested is shown in blue across states, ranging from 20% (dark blue) to 18.7% (light blue). In panel B, the actual premium increase granted by the state regulator is shown in green across states, ranging from 30% (dark green) to 0% (pale green). The data come from the California Rate Guide, and they cover the years 2007–2017.

2.description: Actual to expected LTCI claims for the 15 largest companies The figure shows the actual costs of providing LTCI (x-axis) versus the projected costs of providing LTCI (y-axis) based on approved pricing assumptions reported to NAIC. Moving from the thinnest to the thickest side, each line represents a time-series path for one of the 15 largest insurance companies based on annual premiums sold. The 45-degree red line represents the points on which actual costs are equal to expected costs. The data come from the 1997–2015 annual NAIC Long-Term Care Experience Forms.

3.description: Frequency of applications and approvals over regulator’s election cycle The panels above summarize the total number of rate increase applications received (panel A) versus approved (panel B) by state regulators over their 4-year election cycle. In panel B, we include both approvals of rate increase applications submitted in the same year and approvals of any outstanding applications. The election data have been hand-collected from state websites; the premium change data come from the NAIC Long-Term Care Experience Forms; and the increase request data come from the California Rate Guide. Data in panel A cover the years 1997–2015, and data in panel B cover the years 2007–2017.

4.description: Average rate requests changes across the election cycle The panels summarize the effect of regulator election cycles on premium increase requests sent to the regulator (panel A) and on average premium changes (panel B). Panel A shows a binscatter plot of the probability of a premium increase request getting approved (y-axis) versus the number of years left in the state regulator’s term (x-axis), controlling for state and matched-group fixed effects. Panel B shows a binscatter plot of annual premium changes (y-axis) versus the number of years left in the state regulator’s term (x-axis), controlling for state and year fixed effects. The underlying data contains one observation per policy, company, state, and year (panel A) or company, state, and year (panel B). The election data are hand-collected from state websites, the premium change data come from the NAIC Long-Term Care Experience Forms, and the increase request data come from the California Rate Guide. Data in panel A cover the years 1997–2015, and data in panel B cover the years 2007–2017.

5.description: Political frictions and cash reserves The figures above show cash reserves, or cumulative cash flows (defined as total premiums collected minus total claims paid out), over time depending on states’ political factors. Panel A breaks down these cash reserves based on whether the state has high or low election cycle frictions. High friction states, shown in red, are those where election cycles are estimated to have a larger effect on regulator behavior. Low friction states are shown in blue. Panel B breaks down company cash flows based on regulator stringency, as proxied for by the political affiliation of the regulator. States with Democratic regulators, which are generally more stringent on premium increase requests, are shown in blue, and states with Republican regulators are shown in red. The underlying data come from the California Long-term Care Rate and History Guide, the SERFF online database, and NAIC LTC Experience Reports. The data sample covers the years 1995–2005.

6.description: Effect of regulator behavior on company dropout The panels above summarize the effect of regulators on the supply of insurance. Panel A shows a binscatter plot of the probability of a company dropping out of the LTCI market (y-axis) versus the average premium increase granted by its regulator (x-axis), controlling for state fixed effects. Panel B shows a binscatter plot of the probability of a company dropping out of the LTCI market (y-axis) versus the percent of all premium increase requests that are granted by its regulator (x-axis), controlling for state fixed effects. The underlying data contain one observation per company and state. The premium increase request data come from the California Rate Guide, and company dropouts are proxied for using advertisement filings from the SERFF (The System for Electronic Rates & Forms Filing) online database. In states in which insurance companies are required to file for advertising in order to sell a product, we count a company as dropped in the year that they stop filing for advertising. The data cover the years 2005–2014.
